coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence turn-on immunosensing of HE4 biomarker and ovarian cancer cells based on target-triggered metal-enhanced fluorescence of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Cuiping
Chen, Ruoyu
Wu, Xueqing
Shi, Nian
Duan, Tengfei
Xu, Kai
Huang, Tonghui
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and sensitive detection of tumor biomarkers and cancer cells is of crucial importance for the early diagnosis and prognosis prediction of cancer. The present report describes a target-induced fluorescence enhancement immunosensor that utilizes the optical property of carbon dots (CDs) and the metal-enhanced fluorescence effect (MEF) property of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for the sensitive detection of the cancer biomarker human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) and ovarian cancer cells. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs with a quantum yield of 85.6% were prepared and served as the fluorophore in MEF. The HE4 antibody (Ab) specific to the HE4 antigen was linked covalently to the surface of the synthesized CDs as the capture. The HE4 Ab-conjugated AgNPs (AgNPs-Ab) were prepared and utilized as signal amplification elements. In the presence of the target HE4, composite sandwich structures were formed between the labeled CDs-Ab and AgNPs-Ab, which brought the CDs and AgNPs into proximity, resulting in the fluorescence of CDs enhancement owing to MEF. The intensity of fluorescence enhancement was positively correlated with the HE4 concentration in the clinically important range of 0.01–200 nM with a limit detection of 2.3 pM. Moreover, the immunosensor was also successfully applied to specific fluorescence labeling and quantitative determination of HE4-positive ovarian cancer cells. The proposed target-triggered MEF sensor platform demonstrated high sensitivity, excellent anti-interference ability, along with successful validation in complex biological matrices, providing a new approach for HE4 detection in early diagnosis and therapeutic monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HE4 immunoassay
Metal-enhanced fluorescence effect
Carbon dots
Ovarian-cancer cells
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bioconjugation of synthesized CDs with Anti-HE4
Synthesis of AgNPs and Anti-HE4-labeled AgNPs (AgNPs-Ab)
The MEF mechanism of HE4 detection
Ovarian cancer cells imaging and detection

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81671744 and 81771904 ), Six Talent Peaks of Jiangsu Province ( WSW-051 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Xuzhou ( KC20104 and KC21164 ), and Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Representative TEM image of the synthesized CDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of CDs. (c) Size distribution profile of CDs obtained from TEM images. (d) TEM image of AgNPs. (e) TEM image of AgNP-Ab/CDs-Ab mixture after 100 nM HE4 addition. (f) TEM image of AgNP-Ab/CDs-Ab mixture. The CDs were indicated with red circles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Characterization of CDs. (a) Full XPS spectrum of CDs. (b) The contents of elements in CDs. High-resolution (c) C1s, (d) S2p and (e) N1s spectra of CDs. (f) Typical FTIR spectrum of CDs.

(a) Absorbance spectrum of CDs. The insert shows a photo of CDs solution under a 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation conditions. (c) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and CDs-Ab, respectively. (d) Absorption spectra of the Ab, AgNPs and AgNPs-Ab, respectively.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CD-Ab (curve a), CD-Ab/AgNPs-Ab mixture (curve b), CD-Ab/AgNPs-Ab in the presence of 100 nM HE4 (curve c), and CD-Ab in the presence of 100 nM HE4 (curve d). (b) Effect of AgNPs concentrations on the response of immunosensor system to 100 nM HE4. (c) Effect of incubation time on the response of immunosensor to 100 nM HE4. (d) Fluorescence spectra of the immunosensor in the presence of HE4 with different concentrations. (e) The linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity at 451 nm and the concentrations of HE4. (f) Specificity of immunosensor system for the determination of HE4. From 1 to 11: BSA, TRF, CA-125, human IgG, Glu, Phe, Arg, ATP, complex matrices, HE4 and HE4 in complex matrices, respectively. The concentration of HE4 was 100 nM and the concentrations of other biomolecules were 1.0 μM. The complex matrices contain 1.0 μM of control biomolecules and 100 μM common inorganic ions (Ca2+, Na+, K+, HPO3 2−, Cl− and HCO3 − ions). I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of proteins, respectively. Error bars are standard deviations (n = 3). ∗∗ indicates significant differences (P < 0.001) from values for the samples of 1–9.

Multicolor (blue, green, red) fluorescence imaging of cells. (a) HO-8910 cells and (b) Ea.hy926 cells treated with CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab for 1 h. (c) HO-8910 cells cultured with unmodified CDs/AgNPs under the same conditions. Fluorescent imaging was performed using specific filters with the following excitation (λex) and emission wavelength cut offs (λem): Blue channel: λex = 340–380 nm, λem = 435–485 nm; Green channel: λex = 480/40 nm, λem = 510 nm; Red channel: λex = 515–560 nm, λem = 580 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence images of HO-8910 cells co-stained with DAPI, CellTracker Red CMTPX and nanoparticles. Blue: nucleus stained with DAPI; Green: fluorescence of CDs; Red: cytosol stained with CellTracker Red CMTPX. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra of immunosensor system upon assay different concentrations of HO-8910 cell. (b) The plot and linear fitting of fluorescence intensity at 451 nm vs. the concentration of cells. (c) Specificity of immunosensor system for the determination of HO-8910 cells. (d) The fluorescence intensity at 451 nm detected in different biological matrices. Error bars are standard deviations (n = 3). ∗∗ indicates significant differences (P < 0.001) as compared to the PBS and Ea.hy926 groups. ∗ indicates significant differences (P < 0.01) as compared to the negative control groups.
Schematic illustration of MEF-based immunoassay for HE4 detection.
Recovery results of HE4 in serum samples (n = 3).
Recovery results of HO-8910 cells in serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence turn-on immunosensing of HE4 biomarker and ovarian cancer cells based on target-triggered metal-enhanced fluorescence of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, 221004, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and sensitive detection of tumor biomarkers and cancer cells is of crucial importance for the early diagnosis and prognosis prediction of cancer. The present report describes a target-induced fluorescence enhancement immunosensor that utilizes the optical property of carbon dots (CDs) and the metal-enhanced fluorescence effect (MEF) property of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for the sensitive detection of the cancer biomarker human epididymis protein 4 (HE4) and ovarian cancer cells. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs with a quantum yield of 85.6% were prepared and served as the fluorophore in MEF. The HE4 antibody (Ab) specific to the HE4 antigen was linked covalently to the surface of the synthesized CDs as the capture. The HE4 Ab-conjugated AgNPs (AgNPs-Ab) were prepared and utilized as signal amplification elements. In the presence of the target HE4, composite sandwich structures were formed between the labeled CDs-Ab and AgNPs-Ab, which brought the CDs and AgNPs into proximity, resulting in the fluorescence of CDs enhancement owing to MEF. The intensity of fluorescence enhancement was positively correlated with the HE4 concentration in the clinically important range of 0.01–200 nM with a limit detection of 2.3 pM. Moreover, the immunosensor was also successfully applied to specific fluorescence labeling and quantitative determination of HE4-positive ovarian cancer cells. The proposed target-triggered MEF sensor platform demonstrated high sensitivity, excellent anti-interference ability, along with successful validation in complex biological matrices, providing a new approach for HE4 detection in early diagnosis and therapeutic monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Ovarian carcinoma ranks second among the most common malignant gynecological cancers and is the leading cause of death related to gynecological cancers [1]. Early detection and diagnosis of ovarian carcinoma is of critical importance for the timely treatment of this disease. However, ∼75% of ovarian cancer patients are diagnosed at an advanced stage, for which no effective treatment is available to date [2]. In this context, accurate and sensitive determination of ovarian cancer cells and related biomarkers are imperative for the early diagnosis and prognosis evaluation of ovarian carcinoma. A secretion protein, Human epididymis protein 4 (HE4), which has a low expression in normal/healthy tissues, including the ovaries, is over-expressed in ovarian carcinoma [3,4]. Owing to this differential expression, HE4 protein is considered an excellent target for the diagnosis and treatment of ovarian carcinoma [5]. Several methods are reported for the detection of HE4, including the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [6], enzyme immunoassay (EIA) [7], localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) biosensing [8,9], chemiluminescence immunoassay [10], and electrochemical immunoassay [11]. Nonetheless, challenges such as instability, high cost, nonhomogeneous reaction, and tedious operation remain to be resolved. Developing a detection system that is simple in design, easy to operate, and highly sensitive in the determination of HE4 expression and ovarian cancer cells remains to be a challenging task for the researchers.

Recent studies have reported the effectiveness of fluorescence sensing techniques in the detection of biomarkers and cancer cells, with high sensitivity, design and operation simplicity, and availability of real-time detection [12]. In a typical fluorescence assay, fluorescent labels are used mainly as signal transduction materials that bind directly to the ligand for recognition with target analytes. Therefore, the sensitivity of a fluorescence assay is greatly influenced by the optical properties of the labels used and the recognition abilities of the ligands. Among the family of fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) are attracting considerable attention from the researchers owning to their unique optical properties, excellent water solubility, and intrinsic biocompatibility [13,14]. In recent years, a tremendous effort has been devoted to developing efficient methods for the synthesis of CDs with high quantum yields and exploring their potential applications [15]. Significant progress has been achieved in the CD-based fluorescence assays for metal ions, molecules, temperature, and pH owing to the chemical stability and super optical properties of CDs [16,17]. Generally, the main sensing mechanisms involved are photo-induced electron/energy transfer (PET) [18], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [19,20], and inner-filter effect (IFE) [21]. For instance, Al-Ogaidi et al. proposed a FRET system equipped with a CD-aptamer as the detection probe and AuNPs as the quenchers for the immunosensing of CA125 [20]. Lu's group constructed a sensitive fluorescence sensor platform equipped with CDs for the detection of β-glucuronidase based on IFE [21]. Our group has previously reported the utility of label-free N-doped CDs as selective “on-off-on” probes for the detection and imaging of iron(III) ion and apoferritin based on the charge/energy transfer effect [22]. Despite the appreciable progress, certain problems, such as poor luminescence efficiency, limited signal amplification, and unfavorable background noise, are encountered. Moreover, most of the sensors are based on the signal quenching of CDs and the final “signal-off” phenomenon may also be triggered by the other quenchers or environmental stimuli, leading to false-positive results. Therefore, designing ultrasensitive strategies for improving the detection performance continues to be a challenge.

Owing to the unique localized surface plasmon resonance phenomenon occurring in certain noble metal nanoparticles, such as silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), the fluorescence emission intensity of the fluorophores located in close proximity to these noble metal nanostructures may be significantly enhanced [23]. This phenomenon is known as the metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect, which depends strongly on the size and shape of the metal nanoparticles, as well as on the distance between the fluorophore and the nanoparticle surface [24]. Recently, the MEF-based strategies have been applied successfully in the fluorescence-based sensing platforms to improve the detection sensitivity at ultra-low analyte concentrations. For instance, Choi et al. [25] constructed a MEF biosensor for detecting proteolytic enzymes at as low as femtomolar concentrations, using bifunctional AuNPs in combination with a fluorophore. Zheng et al. [26] demonstrated a novel approach to detect intracellular adenosine triphosphate (ATP) with a detection limit of 35 nM based on MEF triggered by aptamer nanoswitch. The antigen-antibody interaction has been proved to be considerably effective in tuning the distance between the fluorophore and the metal surface as the sandwich-type immuno-reaction binding [27]. Therefore, it is possible to integrate the immunoanalytical methods with the MEF strategy to improve the sensitivity of fluorescence measurements.

The present study reports the development of a signal turn-on immunobiosensing platform that combines the optical property of CDs with the MEF effect characteristic of AgNPs, for the first time, for the detection of HE4 in a complex biological matrix (see Scheme 1 ). The CDs were synthesized with a quantum yield of 85.6% using a one-step solvothermal reaction involving folic acid as the carbon source and thiourea as the nitrogen and sulfur source. The capture HE4 antibody (Ab) specific to the HE4 antigen was linked covalently to the surface of the synthesized CDs through amide conjugation. AgNPs were utilized as signal amplification elements by linking them with Ab. In the presence of the target HE4, a composite sandwich structure were formed between the Ab-labeled AgNPs and the CDs via the antigen-antibody reaction, resulting in the fluorescence of CDs enhancement due to the MEF characteristic of AgNPs. Based on this phenomenon, a target-induced fluorescence turn-on determination of HE4 with high sensitivity and specificity was achieved. The application of the immunosensor in the imaging and quantitative determination of ovarian cancer cells is also described in the present report.

Folic acid, urea, ethylene glycol, silver nitrate (AgNO3), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC∙HCl), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), tri-sodium citrate and 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) were purchased from Sigma (Shanghai, China). The HE4 protein and the Anti-HE4 antibody were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, MA USA). The CellTiter 96 was purchased from Promega (Madison, Wis). CellTracke Red CMTPX was obtained from Yeasen Biotech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was prepared using the Mill-Q system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade. All reagents were used as procured, without any further purification.

The morphology and microstructure of the nanoparticles were examined under a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, 200 KV, USA). The FT-IR spectra were obtained from infrared absorption spectroscopy (Bruker, Germany). The surface states of the CDs were evaluated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out with a Thermo escalab250Xi (Thermo Fished Scientific, USA) using Al Kα X-ray radiation. The fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra were measured using a LS-45/55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) and a UH4150 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The zeta potentials of the nanoparticles were determined using Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, British). The fluorescence images were captured under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Leica DMI 3000B, Germany).

CDs were synthesized using the one-pot solvothermal method. In this method, folic acid (0.2 mmol) and thiourea (2 mmol) were added to the solution of ethylene glycol (5 mL) and H2O (0.5 mL). The resultant solution was heated at 150 °C for 7 h with vigorous stirring. After cooling down to room temperature, the prepared brownish-yellow solution was purified by dialysis for 48 h and then lyophilized to obtain a light-brownish solid of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconjugation of synthesized CDs with Anti-HE4
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-HE4 was conjugated with the synthesized CDs using EDC/NHS as coupling agents. Briefly, EDC∙HCl (10 mg) was mixed into a 1-mL solution of CDs in PBS (1 mg mL−1) for 15 min to activate the carboxyl groups. Subsequently, NHS (5 mg) was mixed into this solution for another 15 min. Finally, the Anti-HE4 (10 μL, 1.789 mg mL−1) was added, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 2 h to allow the reaction to occur. Afterward, the large cross-linked particles in the solution were removed through centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min). The collected supernatant was subjected to centrifugal ultrafiltration (5500 rpm, 20 min) in a Millipore ultrafiltration tube, and the obtained nanoparticles were dissolved with 2 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AgNPs and Anti-HE4-labeled AgNPs (AgNPs-Ab)
ce:section.ce:para:
The AgNPs were synthesized through the NaBH4 reduction of AgNO3 as described previously [28]. Briefly, 0.5 mL of 1% tri-sodium citrate was added to 100 mL of 0.1 mM AgNO3 solution. After stirring for 5 min at room temperature, 5 mL of 1 mM NaBH4 were added drop by drop, and the resulting mixture was stirred for a further 2 h to obtain the bright-yellow solution of AgNPs. The concentration of AgNPs determined using the spherical model [29,30] (equation (1)) was 5.67 nM.
(1)
C =
[ A
g +
]
( 4 / 3 ) π
d 3
× d e n s i t y
o f
A g / a t o m i c
w e i g h t
o f
A g
=
C o n c e n t a r t i o n o f A g + i n t h e p r e c u r s o r s o l u t i o n
t h e n u m b e r o f A g a t o m s i n e a c h A g N P s
Where C is the concentration of AgNP, d is the radius of individual AgNPs. The atomic weight of Ag is 108 g mol−1. The density of Ag is 10,500 kg m−3.

The Anti-HE4 was labeled with the AgNPs by exploiting the electrostatic and covalent interactions between the antibody side chains and the surfaces of the nanoparticles. The AgNPs-Ab was prepared by adding Anti-HE4 (10 μL, 1.789 mg mL−1) into the AgNPs solution (1 mL), followed by oscillation for 6 h at room temperature. Afterward, the obtained AgNPs-Ab was purified by centrifugation to remove the unbound Ab, which was followed by incubation with BSA (w/w: 5%) to ensure there were no nonspecific binding interactions. Excess BSA was removed by centrifugation, and the sediment was re-suspended in PBS solution and stored at 4 °C.

In a typical assay, 10 μL of the analyte was mixed with 50 μL of CDs-Ab (10 μg mL−1 in PBS) and 10 μL of AgNPs-Ab (2.8 nM). After incubation at 37 °C for 20 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured. The sensitivity of the assay was determined using serial standard dilutions of HE4 (blank, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 10, 50, 100, and 200 nM). In the specificity tests, bovine serum albumin (BSA), transferrin (TRF), immunoglobulin G (lgG), cancer antigen 125 (CA-125), glucose (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), arginine (Arg) and ATP were analyzed. Student's t-test was applied to analysis the significant difference between different groups. All tests were two-sided, and a p value of less than 0.05 was considered to indicate a significant difference.

Recovery of the assay was determined using the standard HE4-spiked serum samples and equation (2):
(2) Recovery = 100% × (measured concentration/spiked concentration)

The cells were seeded into a 24-well plate and incubated overnight in a complete cell culture medium at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells were treated with AgNPs-Ab (2.8 nM Ag) for 1 h, followed by incubation with CDs-Ab (containing 100 μg mL−1 of CDs) for another 1 h. Then, the cells were washed with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) three times, The unmodified CD and CD-Ab (100 μg mL−1)-treated cells were used as controls. For intracellular distribution studies, the treated cells were co-stained with CellTracke Red CMTPX as described in the manufacturer instructions manual for cytosol staining and DAPI for cell nuclei staining. The fluorescence images were captured under a Leica DMI3000 microscope.

For cell detection, the treated cells were harvested through centrifugation (1000 rpm for 5 min) and diluted to different concentrations. Then, the fluorescence spectra of each sample was measured. To evaluate the sensing performance of the fluorescence detection system in complex biological matrices, urine and serum were mixed with 5.0 × 104 cell mL−1 of HO-8910 cells. Then AgNPs-Ab and CD-Ab were added into the complex samples to identify the target cells.
The recovery experiments were conducted at the above-stated conditions for the HO-8910 cells-spiked serum samples. The values of recovery were calculated using equation (2).

The CDs were synthesized through the carbonization of folic acid and thiourea in ethylene glycol at 150 °C for 7 h. The TEM images revealed that the synthesized CDs were suitably mono-dispersed, had a narrow size distribution ranging between 1.7 nm and 4.9 nm with a mean particle size of 2.8 nm (Fig. 1 a and c). The high-resolution TEM image of the synthesized CDs in Fig. 1b depicts an average interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite [31].

The composition of the CDs was identified by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The XPS spectrum of the synthesized CDs showed five peaks at 164, 227, 284, 400, and 533 eV, which attributed to the bonding energies of S2p, S2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 a) [32]. The results clearly demonstrated that the synthesized CDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur elements, and the contents of these elements in the samples were 11.45% (S), 60% (C), 13.81% (N), and 14.75% (O), respectively (Fig. 2b). The highly-resolved C1s XPS spectrum revealed that carbon presented three different chemical environments, corresponding to the sp2 carbon (C–C, 284.2 eV), sp3 carbon (C–N, C–S, and C–O, 285.3 eV), and carbonyl carbon (CO, 287 eV) (Fig. 2c). The highly-resolved N1s spectrum contained three peaks at 399.8, 400.6, and 401.7 eV, which represented pyridinic C–N–C, pyrrolic C2–N–H, and graphitic N or N–H bands, respectively (Fig. 2d). The S2p spectrum contained three distinct peaks at 162.8, 164.1, and 168.0 eV, which were attributed to S2p3/2 of thiophene, and S2p1/2 of thiophene, and SO bonding, respectively (Fig. 2e) [33]. These results indicated that N and S elements were doped into the synthesized CDs.

Furthermore, the FT-IR analysis was performed to investigate the surface functional groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2f, the peaks at 1703 cm−1 and 1607 cm−1 corresponded to the carboxylate symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively. The absorption bands at 1536 cm−1 and 1297 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of C–O–C group and NH group, respectively. The absorption peak at 1415 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibrations of CN. The bands at 1085 cm−1 and 649 cm−1 were associated with CS and C–S stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1042 cm−1 and 882 cm−1 represented the C–H and C–C stretching, respectively. The broad absorption bands in the 3100–3500 cm−1 region represented the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The results indicated that hydroxyl, carboxylic, and amine groups were abundant on the surface of the as-synthesized CDs, which were responsible for the CDs’ good water solubility and high affinity to biomolecules.

The optical properties of the synthesized CDs were characterized using the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The aqueous solutions of the CDs exhibited two absorption peaks around 240 nm and 350 nm, which were probably due to the π-π∗ transition of CC bond and n-π∗ transition of the N and S-doped surface state region, respectively (Fig. 3a) [34]. Upon excitation at 365 nm under an ultraviolet lamp, the solution of CDs emitted strong blue fluorescence that was clearly visible to the naked eye (inset in Fig. 3 a). The emission peak of the CDs displayed a red-shift from 412 nm to 515 nm, when the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 440 nm (Fig. 3b). Such an excitation–dependent emission behavior is one of the most prominent features of carbon-based fluorescence materials, which may be due to the variation in the particle sizes and the distribution of surface energy traps of the particles [35]. The maximum emission wavelength was observed at 448 nm under excitation at 360 nm (Fig. S1). Quantum yield was found to be 85.6%, which is much higher compared to the recently reported values [34,36]. After modification with Ab, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-Ab exhibited a emission peak at 451 nm, 3 nm red-shifted with a slight decrease in the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3c) and quantum yield (72.4%), which might be caused by a quantum effect [37,38]. Meanwhile, zeta potential of CDs and CDs-Ab were investigated. The zeta potential value of CDs in neutral aqueous solution changed from −21.44 mV to −15.6 mV after modification. This observation indicated the successful labeling of Ab with CDs.

The CDs exhibited excellent photostability. Under continuous irradiation with a UV lamp (365 nm), the fluorescence of CDs increased within 2 h, and then remained stable for 15 h (Fig. S2), which indicated that CDs have higher photobleaching-resistance. The observed fluorescence enhancement induced by the irradiation of UV light in the first 2 h may be correlated to the surface structural changes upon photoinduced reactions [39]. It might lead to the generation of more defect energy trap states, which contribute to an increase of the radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs and thus induce the fluorescence enhancement. Moreover, the CDs were highly stable in high ionic strength solutions (Fig. S3), and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs stored in a buffer at room temperature remained almost unchanged even after one month (Fig. S4). The stability of the CDs ensured the stability of the proposed immunosensor.

AgNPs, the key constituents of the proposed immunosensor, were synthesized and characterized by TEM and UV–vis spectroscopy. As visible in the representative TEM images in Fig. 1d, the as-synthesized AgNPs had a nearly spherical shape with an average diameter of 16.3 nm (Fig. S5). The AgNPs exhibited a strong absorption centered at 395 nm (Fig. 3d). After conjugation with Ab, the AgNPs-Ab showed a 7 nm red-shift in absorbance and broadening of the bands compared to bare AgNPs, which could be due to the change in the refractive index caused by a protein layer formation on the surface of nanoparticles [40]. Moreover, the typical absorbance peak at 277 nm corresponding to Ab was observed in the AgNP-Ab sample, indicating the successful labeling of Ab with AgNPs. The zeta potential of AgNPs and AgNPs-Ab were −31.6 mV and −23.2 mV, respectively. The difference in the zeta potential further suggested the successful binding of Ab with AgNPs. The FTIR spectrum of the AgNPs-Ab showed typical amide I (1646 cm−1) and amide II (1519 cm−1) bands (Fig. S6) [41], also suggesting the attachment of Ab.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The MEF mechanism of HE4 detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is well established that the fluorophores in proximity to AgNPs experience a fluorescence enhancement due to increased their radioactive decay rate [42]. The spectral overlap between fluorophore emission and plasmonic resonance of AgNPs is required for effective fluorescence enhancement [43]. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited broad emission and absorption spectra, which provides a good overlap with the absorption LSPR spectra of the AgNPs, facilitating fluorescence enhancement. When the target HE4 antigen was present in the immunoassay, the CDs-Ab/HE4 complex was formed due to the strong antibody-antigen interaction, and subsequently, the AgNPs-Ab could assemble with this CDs-Ab/HE4 complex through sandwich-type immuno-binding to form a hybrid CDs-Ab/HE4/AgNP-Ab nano-composite. Owing to the sandwich structure, the CDs are in close proximity to the AgNPs and thereby facilitating the MEF effect, which ultimately enhanced the fluorescence emission. As depicted in Fig. 4 a (curve c), after the addition of 100 nM HE4, over 2.1-fold fluorescence enhancement was observed. The TEM images depict the satellite assembly formed with multiple CDs surrounding the AgNPs (Fig. 1e).

The control experiment involved directly mixing the CDs-Ab with AgNPs-Ab, and the AgNPs and CDs were distributed randomly (Fig. 1f). This phenomenon could be attributed to the electrostatic interactions between CDs-Ab and AgNPs-Ab. The zeta-potentials of CDs-Ab and AgNPs-Ab were −15.6 mV and −23.2 mV, respectively. In the absence of the HE4 antigen, the CDs-Ab are far away from the magnetic field generated by the plasma resonance of AgNPs due to the electrostatic repulsion among them, and therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ab is not affected (Fig. 4a, curve b). The results demonstrated that HE4 could effectively mediate the assembly of CDs and AgNPs, thereby inducing the MEF effect between these two nanoparticles. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of CDs-Ab upon the addition of HE4 were also investigated, and it was observed that the fluorescence of CDs-Ab was scarcely influenced by HE4 under the same conditions (Fig. 4a, curve d). This result further implied the feasibility of the “switch-on” fluorescent sensing system with a higher signal-to-background ratio compared to CDs-Ab due to the efficient MEF capabilities of AgNPs in the present study.

In order to achieve the highest possible detection sensitivity, the analytical conditions, including the concentration of AgNPs incubation time and pH were optimized systematically. Since the AgNPs conferred the fluorescence enhancement effect, the concentration of AgNPs-Ab could affect the assay performance. In order to evaluate the impact of AgNPs-Ab on the analytical performance of the assay system, the effect of the concentration of AgNPs-Ab on HE4 sensing was investigated by adding different concentrations of AgNPs-Ab to the CDs-Ab solution containing 100 nM of HE4. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity enhanced with the increase in the concentration of AgNPs-Ab until the concentration reached 2.8 nM. Afterward, the fluorescence signals decreased when the concentration of AgNPs-Ab exceeded 3 nM, which could be due to the inner-filter effect of AgNPs [44]. Therefore, 2.8 nM was considered as the optimum AgNPs-Ab concentration for further experiments.

Investigation of the HE4-induced fluorescence enhancement kinetics of the immunosensor in the presence of 100 nM HE4 was performed using fluorescence spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity at 451 nm intensified with time of incubation and reached a plateau after 10 min. Thus, the incubation time of 10 min was chosen for the detection of HE4 in the subsequent experiments. The current method for the detection of HE4 was much faster than several other methods that require incubation time of more than 20 min [6–10].

The pH value also had a great effect on assay behavior of the immunosensor because too low pH or high pH condition will influence the activity of Abs. The effect of pH on the immunosensor for HE4 detection was examined within the range of 5.5–8.0. As shown in Fig. S6, the pH values between 6.8 and 7.4 were beneficial to the strong fluorescent response, indicating the probe was suitable for biological sample analysis.

To evaluate the analytical performance of the immunosensor developed in the present study, the spectral changes for the fluorescence detection system upon the addition of different concentrations of HE4 were investigated under the optimal conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence signal increased with the increasing concentration of HE4 from 0 to 200 nM. In the fluorescence intensity at 451 nm versus HE4 concentration plot, the fluorescence intensity displayed a good linear relationship with HE4 concentration for a wide range of 0.01–200 nM (Fig. 4e). The regression equation was C/I = 0.00243C + 0.00112 (I represents the fluorescence intensity and C denotes the concentration of HE4). The correlation coefficient (r2) of the linear fit was 0.9998. The estimated limit of detection, based on three times the signal-to-noise level, was approximately 2.3 pM, which was far below the threshold for HE4 (38 pM) in normal human serum [45]. The result demonstrated that the immunosensor is sensitive, with considerable potential for clinical applications.

Sensing selectivity is another important feature of the sensors. To assess the specificity of the proposed immunosensor, several biomolecules, including BSA, TRF, CA-125, human IgG, Glu, Phe, Arg and ATP, were selected as controls. The 100 nM of HE4 and 1 μM of interference substances were measured using the proposed immunosensor. As visible in Fig. 4f, HE4 induced a significant enhancement of fluorescence (increased by approximately 95% compared to the original value, p < 0.001), while the other tested proteins induced negligible fluorescence change (p > 0.05), which reflected the high specificity of the proposed immunosensor for HE4. The mixture samples containing 100 nM of HE4, 1 μM of control biomolecules (BSA, TRF, CA-125, IgG, Glu, Phe, Arg and ATP) and 100 μM common inorganic ions (Ca2+, Na+, K+, HPO3 2−, Cl− and HCO3 − ions) were utilized to examine the anti-interference ability of the proposed immunosensor, and the results revealed that interference from the interfering biomolecules and ions was minimal even when the concentration of the interfering substances was ten times higher than HE4. The statistical result showed no significant difference (p > 0.05) compared with the results obtained in PBS solution. These results demonstrate that the specificity of the sensing system relies mainly on the biospecific recognition by antibodies. The sandwich technology, together with the MEF effect, further enhanced the selectivity of the proposed sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ovarian cancer cells imaging and detection
ce:section.ce:para: A possible application of the immunosensor as an agent for HO-8910 ovarian cancer cells imaging was also explored. As depicted in the imaging results, the HO-8910 cells treated with CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab emitted stronger blue, green, and red fluorescence signal under different excitation conditions (Fig. 5 ). This result agreed well with the results of fluorescence spectrometric analysis. On the other hand, no photoluminescence observed at the nucleus might be because the HE4 antigen exists mainly in the cancer cell membrane and cytoplasm [46] and can effectively deliver the nanoparticles into the cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis [47,48]. The negative controls of HO-8910 cells incubated with unmodified CDs and AgNPs were studied. Only a weak and randomly-distributed fluorescence was observed at the membrane of the cells, which might have been caused by the nonspecific pinocytosis of the nanoparticles into the cells. Moreover, the HE4-negative Ea.hy926 normal cells were used as the negative control to verify the specificity of the probes, and it was observed that the Ea.hy926 cells treated with CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab exhibited no significant fluorescence, certifying the high specificity of the probes.

To get some insights into the intracellular distribution of the nanoparticles, the treated HO-8910 cells were co-stained by DAPI (blue) and CellTracker Red CMTPX Dye (red) to visualize cell nucleus and cytoplasm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , green fluorescence of specifically uptake CDs was clearly visible within the inner part of the red stained cytosol whereas no obvious fluorescence nanoparticles can be detected at the nucleus. Particularly, the fluorescence signals were more intense in the HO-8910 cells treated by CD-Ab compared to the CDs treated group, confirming the targeting of Ab on the surface of CDs to the HO-8910 cells. Besides, a significant fluorescence enhancement in cytoplasm could be observed for the CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab co-treated HO-8910 cells compared to CDs-Ab and CDs-treated cell groups, which indicated that the CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab immunosensor system could be used for sensing of HE4 in cell cytoplasm via target-triggered MEF mechanism, and produced an outstanding fluorescence enhancement in cell imaging.

The highly sensitive fluorescence assay and the high specificity of the proposed immunosensor to HE4-positive ovarian cancer cells indicated that the strategy is directly applicable for ovarian cancer cells detection. To assess the sensitivity of our assay, suspensions of HO-8910 cells at different concentrations were prepared by diluting a stock suspension of these cells. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing the HO-8910 cells concentration, and a linear relationship was observed between the fluorescence intensity at 451 nm and the concentration of the HO-8910 cells in the range of 1.02 × 104 to 2.56 × 106 cell mL−1 (Fig. 7b). The regression equation was I = 0.124C + 42.4 (r2 = 0.9911), and the detection limit was as low as 196 cells mL−1. In the meantime, the same process for the assay of HO-8910 cells was performed with the CDs-Ab. As shown in Fig. S7, the HO-8910 cells treated with CDs-Ab showed weaker fluorescence signal compared to CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab treated cells. The linear range of this was from 1.72 × 105 to 2.3 × 106 cell mL−1 (I = 0.074C + 38.99, r2 = 0.9959) with the limit of 6918 cells mL−1 (Fig. S8). The limit of the assay method for HO-8910 cells detection using CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab system is ∼35 times lower than using CDs-Ab directly. The result shows that the MEF effect of AgNPs can greatly enhance the sensitivity of fluorescence and decrease the detection limit.

To further evaluate the selectivity of our assay, the proposed immunosensor was incubated with the Ea.hy926 cells under the same conditions, and the fluorescence of the cell suspensions was investigated. The fluorescence intensity change ratio at 451 nm (ΔI, defined here as the absolute of intensity change ratio, i.e., ΔI = (I − I0)/I0), I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of cells before and after treated with CDs-Ab/AgNPs-Ab, respectively) in the HO-8910 ovarian cancer cells (ΔI = 8.80 at 2.56 × 106 cell mL−1) was 13.54 times higher than that of Ea.hy926 cells (ΔI = 0.65 at 3.14 × 106 cell mL−1) (Fig. 7c). And there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity when the concentration of the Ea.hy926 cells increased from 2.1 × 104 to 3.14 × 106 cell mL−1 (Fig. 7b). The differences in the fluorescence responses between the cancer cells and normal cells could be due to the different levels of HE4 protein expression on the surface of these cells. The high level of HE4 protein expression in HO-8910 ovarian cancer cells might have resulted in the effective phagocytosis of nanoparticles.

To investigate the immunosensor's applicability and reliability in complex biological sample, urine and serum were mixed with HO-8910 cells and processed assay. As shown in Fig. 7d, the HO-8910 cells-positive urine and serum samples generate a significant fluorescence enhancement compared to their corresponding negative controls (p < 0.01). This is consistent with the results obtained in PBS solution. The results suggested that the immunosensor has a excellent anti-interference ability in complex biological samples, which provide a promising prospect for early disease diagnosis.

The inherent cytotoxicity of CDs and AgNPs was evaluated using the MTS assay. The cell viabilities of the HO-8910 and Ea.hy926 cells were evaluated after 48 h of incubation with CDs or AgNPs at different concentrations. As depicted in Fig. S9, there was little effect of CDs on the cell viability under the cellar imaging condition where the concentration of CDs was 0.1 mg mL−1. The cell survival rates for both cell lines were over 95%, even at the CD concentration of up to 6 mg mL−1 or at the AgNPs concentration of 5 nM, indicating that the low toxicity of the synthesized nanoparticles for both normal cells and tumor cells and rendering them as particularly promising for cell imaging studies.

In the present study, a functional and innovative combination of the optical property of CDs and the MEF effect feature of AgNPs was employed to conduct the fluoroimmunoassay for HE4 and ovarian cancer cells. The fluorescent detection system demonstrated a highly sensitive and selective target-triggered “turn-on” fluorescence response toward HE4 over other proteins and common ions, which provided a novel strategy for the determination of HE4. In addition, the immunosensor achieved the selective multicolor fluorescence imaging of the ovarian cancer cells and detected the cancer cell concentrations as low as 196 cells mL−1. Notably, the immunoassay developed in the present study is convenient in operation, time-saving, and demonstrates good robustness and specificity in the analysis of blood samples. The proposed sensing strategy may be adapted for the detection of various other biomarkers and cancer cells with suitable capture antibodies, thereby holding great potential for reliable point-of-care diagnostics.

Cuiping Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ruoyu Chen: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Data curation. Xueqing Wu: Investigation, Data curation. Nian Shi: Validation, Formal analysis. Tengfei Duan: Visualization, Software. Kai Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Tonghui Huang: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81671744 and 81771904), Six Talent Peaks of Jiangsu Province (WSW-051), Science and Technology Planning Project of Xuzhou (KC20104 and KC21164), and Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.339160.
